questions.question: Will VIX close above 30 in the next 42 days?

source_markets.question: Will the S&P 500 Index gain at least 5% on any day in Q1?

Will the number of unemployment rate exceeds 10% (monthly BLS); S&P 500 declines more than 30% from its closing level on Issuance; Zillow Home Value Index declines more than 10% YoY in any of: NYC, LA, San Francisco, Chicago, Houston, Phoenix; labor share of gross domestic income (GDI) first-release value for any quarter falls below 50%; CPI-U (All items, not seasonally-adjusted) YoY falls below 0% in any monthly release during before July 2028 be above 2?
Will the S&P 500 Index lose at least 3% on any day in Q1?
Will the S&P 500 Index lose at least 5% on any day in Q1?

questions.reasoning: The market-implied probability for a VIX spike above 30 in the next 42 days is estimated at 8% (5-15% range). There is no direct market for this specific VIX threshold. We rely on proxy evidence from S&P 500 volatility markets, which currently show extremely low expectations for significant downside moves (e.g., <1% probability of 5% daily losses). The low liquidity and lack of direct instruments necessitate a cautious, low-confidence forecast.

questions.question: Will crude oil settle above $90 in June 2026?

source_markets.question: Will Crude Oil (CL) settle over $90 on the final trading day of June 2026?
Will Crude Oil (CL) settle over $60 on the final trading day of June 2026?
Will WTI Crude Oil (WTI) hit (HIGH) $90 in June?
Will WTI Crude Oil (WTI) hit (HIGH) $150 in June?
Will the WTI crude oil settlement price be above 90.99 USD/Bbl on May 26, 2026?
OPEC dissolves in 2026?

questions.reasoning: The market consensus for WTI Crude Oil settling above $90 in June 2026 is approximately 50.5%. Direct markets (Polymarket) show a tight range between 49% and 51% for the $90 threshold. While proxy data from Kalshi suggests a slightly higher probability (59%) for a late May settlement, the overall evidence remains balanced. The low probability of OPEC dissolution (7.5%) suggests market participants expect current supply-side management to persist, anchoring the price expectations. Confidence is moderate due to the thin liquidity in specific high-price threshold markets and the inherent volatility of energy commodities.

Given that a 10% rise from current levels would require a significant price appreciation, and the available proxy markets for these high-threshold price targets are trading at near-zero probabilities, the likelihood of gold rising more than 10% within the next 42 days is estimated to be negligible. The forecast relies on proxy markets which suggest the market does not anticipate a breakout of this magnitude in the immediate term.

questions.question: Will the Strait of Hormuz reopen to regular traffic by June 30, 2026?

source_markets.question: Will the 7-day moving average of transit calls through the Strait of Hormuz as reported by the IMF PortWatch be above 60 before June 1, 2026?
Will the 7-day moving average of transit calls through the Strait of Hormuz as reported by the IMF PortWatch be above 60 before July 1, 2026?
Will the 7-day moving average of transit calls through the Strait of Hormuz as reported by the IMF PortWatch be above 60 before June 15, 2026?
Will the 7-day moving average of transit calls through the Strait of Hormuz as reported by the IMF PortWatch be above 60 before August 1, 2026?
US x Iran permanent peace deal by June 30, 2026?
US x Iran ceasefire extended by April 22, 2026?

questions.reasoning: The market consensus for the Strait of Hormuz reopening to regular traffic (defined as a 7-day moving average of transit calls above 60) by June 30, 2026, is approximately 46%. This is anchored by the Kalshi market for July 1, 2026, which shows a 46.5% probability. While the June 15 market is lower (14.5%), the probability increases significantly by August 1 (65.5%), suggesting the market anticipates a gradual normalization process throughout the summer. The proxy market for a US-Iran peace deal (58.5%) provides secondary support for a potential de-escalation, though the failure of the April ceasefire extension (0.2%) highlights the volatility of the current diplomatic environment.
